verdicts.note: Direction is right ('spending problem, not a revenue problem' [S2][S4]), but -2 = fully 'restraint before any new spending' ignores dossier counter-evidence: nine years as Capital Budget ranking member touting over $32M secured for district projects and praising the capital budget as 'the only bipartisan, unanimous budget' [S3][S4]. An appropriator who campaigns on delivering public investment is not at the full restraint pole; the mix warrants -1 medium.

Score is airtight, but the highlight 'endorsement set unusual for a Republican' imports evaluative framing from the dossier's scoring notes; a neutral highlight should just list the endorsements [S4].

Direction and magnitude hold (millionaire's tax is the pole_b description verbatim), but the explicit progressive-revenue evidence rests on S3 alone; S1 supports only vague 'tax fairness and inflation relief'. A single-source explicit statement is medium, not high, per the guide.

The axis is carbon pricing/clean-energy transition vs energy affordability. Her evidence is water conservation, data-center water demand, and anti-fracking [S2][S3] — environmental-protection positions mapped onto a carbon-policy axis by indirect inference. The basis itself concedes 'no carbon-pricing position stated'. Direction is plausible but this is a low-confidence inference.

Term limits and voting-at-16 [S2] are genuine pole_b structural-reform proposals, but the axis's core is money in politics (PAC refusal, dark-money bans), on which the dossier is silent. +2 = fully reform-the-system overstates two adjacent proposals; +1 fits.
